channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Prices Rise on Possible Deal to Suspend Attacks in Gulf
item.description: Oil prices edged higher after an American official said the U.S. and Iran had agreed to halt attacks in a key shipping lane, raising hopes that hostilities would not escalate.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Oil Markets Edge Higher After Days of Attacks in Persian Gulf
item.description: Oil prices inched up on Sunday evening, while the S&P 500 futures market was little changed.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Philippe Stern, Heir to the Patek Philippe Watch Brand, Dies at 88
item.description: In an era of electronic timepieces, he marketed $40,000 handmade watches as status symbols, breathing new life into a struggling industry.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Philippe Stern in 1992. He was the third generation of his family to run the luxury watchmaker Patek Philippe and helped the Swiss watch industry survive the “quartz crisis.”

item.title: ‘Supergirl’ Falters at the Box Office, Testing DC Studios’ Reboot
item.description: Overall, ticket sales for the weekend were up 21 percent from last year. But the expensive “Supergirl” debuted as a disappointing second, with an estimated $38 million.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: How Polymarket Traders Revolted Over One Silly Syllable
item.description: How one silly syllable ignited a bitter dispute over the nature of reality on Polymarket.
item.dc:creator: David Segal

item.title: Advertisers Are Good at Getting Human Attention. Can They Stand Out to A.I.?
item.description: At Cannes Lions, marketers grappled with how to influence A.I. chatbots.
item.dc:creator: Marty Swant
item.media:credit: Shane Anthony Sinclair/Getty Images for Cannes Lions
item.media:description: Denise Dresser, the chief revenue officer at OpenAI, this week during a session at the Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity called “Advertising in the Age of A.I.”

item.title: In Strait of Hormuz, Renewed Strikes Threaten to Set Back Shipping Recovery
item.description: The attacks came after traffic through the Strait of Hormuz had reached the highest levels since the start of the U.S. war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Iran has tried to establish formal control over shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, something it did not have before.

item.title: Colette Shulman, Soviet Analyst With On-the-Ground Insights, Dies at 94
item.description: She arrived in Moscow in 1955, two years after Stalin’s death, and spent several years as a journalist covering major events and ordinary Soviet lives.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Chaos Came to CBS News. What’s in Store for CNN?
item.description: The tech scion David Ellison is close to completing a merger that would put the legacy broadcaster and the 24-hour cable news network under the same roof.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:description: Turmoil at CBS News around “60 Minutes” alarmed journalists at CNN, which Paramount would acquire in a merger with Warner Bros. Discovery.

item.title: How Much Have Airfares Risen With High Jet Fuel Costs?
item.description: While prices are up overall, there’s a surprising pattern depending on your destination.
item.dc:creator: Ben Blatt and Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: In Colorado, the Long Aftermath of a Fire Feels Political
item.description: When the Trump administration rejected a request for federal assistance to a fire-ravaged part of western Colorado, residents debated which political party was to blame.
item.dc:creator: Kurtis Lee and Matthew DeFeo
item.media:description: The Lee fire burned about 137,800 acres last year in Rio Blanco County, Colo.

item.title: Saks Emerges From Bankruptcy With a New Plan and Corporate Name
item.description: The retailer, renamed Exemplar Luxury Group, is ditching anything that isn’t focused on high-end department store shopping.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Photographs by Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Geoffroy van Raemdonck, chief executive of Exemplar Luxury Group, the renamed parent company of Saks Global, and, right, the interior of the Saks store in Manhattan.

item.title: Uber Enacts Stricter Background Checks for Drivers
item.description: The changes come after The New York Times revealed that the ride-hailing giant approved drivers with many types of criminal convictions, including violent felonies.
item.dc:creator: Emily Steel
item.media:credit: Joseph Seif for The New York Times
item.media:description: Uber is expanding the list of criminal offenses that permanently disqualify a driver in the United States.

item.title: Trump Threatens to Impose 100% Tariff on European Countries Over Tech Taxes
item.description: The president claimed the tariffs would override a trade deal with the European Union, which European officials finalized just days ago.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: It’s unclear how soon President Trump could impose any of the new tariffs he threatened on Friday.

item.title: Congressional Panel Investigating Epstein Subpoenas Leon Black in Unusual Escalation
item.description: The panel’s Republican chairman said he was moved to act after the private equity mogul repeatedly declined to discuss nondisclosure agreements at a closed-door hearing.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein, Jessica Silver-Greenberg and Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:description: Leon Black, the former chief of Apollo Global Management, arrives to testify at a closed-door interview with the House Oversight Committee in Washington, D.C., on Friday.

item.title: Best Investments Over the Last 100 Years? Almost All Are Tech Companies.
item.description: What’s most surprising is that Tesla and SpaceX have entered that elite group. A vast majority of companies weren’t worth owning, a long-running study shows.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: Americans Are Feeling Better About the Economy, Consumer Survey Shows
item.description: Consumer sentiment rose in recent weeks after plummeting early this year as energy costs soared.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A Gulf station in Austin, Texas, on Thursday. Consumers are feeling better about gas prices as they ease.

item.title: In Europe, Tesla Sales Are Rising Despite Views on Elon Musk
item.description: Price cuts and low-interest-rate loans are luring buyers, including people offended by the company’s chief executive.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: A Tesla Supercharger station in Germany. Tesla’s sales have rebounded sharply in Europe alongside sales of all electric cars.

item.title: Oil Prices Return to Prewar Levels After Four Months
item.description: The cost of crude has become a real-time barometer of the Iran war’s toll on the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt, Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Small Plane Crashes Into Tallest Building in Beijing
item.description: Crowds in the capital’s central business district fled as the crash sent debris falling into the streets below.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher, Meaghan Tobin and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:description: The damaged exterior of Citic Tower, also known as China Zun, in Beijing, China on Friday.

item.title: Still Sending Checks in the Mail? Stop, Experts Say
item.description: If you must, keep an eye on your account statements, experts say. Thieves steal checks from mailboxes, then alter the checks with fraudulent names.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Apple, Micron, OpenAI and A.I.’s Rough Summer
item.description: Rising memory prices, more expensive iPads and a longer wait for OpenAI to go public: The sector that has driven markets skyward is hitting turbulence.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Is Sam Altman, OpenAI’s C.E.O., in no hurry to take his artificial intelligence lab public? That prospect is weighing on investors and Wall Street.

item.title: Kalshi to Become a World Cup Partner as It Pursues More Growth
item.description: The prediction market is betting that the association with the soccer tournament will help bolster betting volumes as it works toward an I.P.O.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:description: Kalshi is joining ADI Predictstreet as an official FIFA prediction market partner.

item.title: Ryanair Stops Charging for Parents to Sit With Their Children
item.description: The airline said it had “reluctantly” changed course after a British regulator began investigating whether its fees were illegal.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:description: Ryanair was the only major airline operating in Britain that imposed this type of fee, according to a regulator.

item.title: How a Jet Fuel Refinery Is Scrambling After the Iran War Oil Shock
item.description: For a mega-refinery in Ulsan, South Korea, a top exporter of jet fuel to the West Coast of the United States and other places, weaning off Middle Eastern oil is no small feat.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Chang W. Lee
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
item.description: It is the centerpiece of President Trump’s drive to make more chips in the United States, but the company still has a long way to go before it can be called a complete turnaround.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle

item.title: The High School Pipeline to South Korea’s Chip-Making Fortunes
item.description: Huge memory-chip profits from the global A.I. boom have increased interest in semiconductor factory work. But behind the hype are uncertain job prospects.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:credit: Tina Hsu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A class about diffusion equipment operation taught by Kang Soo Jin.

item.title: Stocks in Asia Tumble as A.I. Uncertainty Jolts Tech Shares
item.description: Crude prices hover at prewar levels, as trading remains volatile.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
item.description: The A.I. company’s advisers are pushing its chief executive, Sam Altman, to move slowly after SpaceX’s stock has been volatile and as the start-up grapples with financial challenges.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Mike Isaac
item.media:description: Sam Altman, the chief executive of OpenAI, has pushed for a trillion-dollar I.P.O.

item.title: Movement Through Strait of Hormuz Is Halted After Attack on Cargo Ship
item.description: The strike on a vessel near the coast of Oman prompted a U.N. agency to pause an evacuation of ships from the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, early this month.

item.title: Medical Journal Retracts Study Claiming Cancer Therapy Is More Effective When Given in the Morning
item.description: In a notice flagging a series of problems with a clinical trial, the journal Nature Medicine said its editors “no longer have confidence in the integrity of the results.”
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: The journal Nature Medicine retracted a study that found a drastic benefit when cancer therapy was administered in the morning.

item.title: SpaceX IPO Shows How Investors in Public Companies Are Losing Power
item.description: SpaceX has joined a growing cohort of companies that use dual class share structures to ensure many shareholders have little say in how they are run.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni and Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bret Johnsen, chief financial officer of SpaceX, celebrates with investors and company members at the Nasdaq the day of the company’s initial public offering.

item.title: Chemours to Pay $450M in First Federal PFAS Settlement
item.description: The settlement addresses the dumping of PFAS “forever chemicals,” which have been linked to cancer and other risks, by Chemours in several states.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Ed Kashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Chemours plant in Fayetteville, N.C. Under a proposed settlement, Chemours would pay fines for discharging PFAS from plants in North Carolina, New Jersey and West Virginia.

item.title: Apple Raises Prices on Macs and iPads Amid the A.I. Boom
item.description: The tech giant cited the soaring costs of memory and storage chips as it increased prices more than $200 on some devices.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Even with a sprawling supply chain, Apple is not immune to the global shortage of chips.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Against Bayer Alleging Roundup Weedkiller Caused Cancer
item.description: The court’s decision is likely to determine the future of thousands of lawsuits against Bayer, which manufactures the weedkiller, over similar claims.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:description: Roundup, which was created in the 1970s, is one of the most popular weedkillers in the world.

item.title: Marianne Lake, a Potential Dimon Successor, Leaves JPMorgan
item.description: Another potential successor, Marianne Lake, is leaving JPMorgan, as the longstanding chief executive enters his third decade atop the bank.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:credit: Carly Zavala for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan Chase’s chief executive since 2006, has said the plan for his successor is “up to God and the board.”

item.title: Inflation Accelerated in May as Iran War Pushed Up Prices
item.description: A closely watched measure of inflation ticked up as the conflict in the Middle East inflated energy prices.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gasoline prices are up more than 30 percent since the start of the war in Iran.

item.title: Iran Strikes Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Undermining Efforts to Restore Traffic
item.description: The attack, confirmed by U.S. and Iranian officials, came after Tehran threatened to disrupt shipping in the waterway and as Washington sought regional support for its peace agreement.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Farnaz Fassihi, Jenny Gross and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels off the coast of Oman near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday.

item.title: The Taylor Swift Wedding and a Hot New York Summer
item.description: Should Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce tie the knot in New York City, their nuptials would be another economic event for the Big Apple.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: New York and fans of Taylor Swift are buzzing over the prospect of Swift and Travis Kelce getting married in the city.

item.title: Larry Sanger, a Wikipedia Founder, Is Barred From Editing on the Site
item.description: A vote to bar Larry Sanger indefinitely from the volunteer community of Wikipedia came days after he submitted a proposal on “intellectual diversity.”
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Ulli Winkler/ullstein bild, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Larry Sanger said that Wikipedia was “one of the most effective organs of establishment propaganda in history.”

item.title: Big Companies Aim to Ease A.I. Transition for American Workers
item.description: OpenAI, Anthropic, Amazon and Microsoft have signed on to an effort led by Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gina Raimondo, a former commerce secretary and Rhode Island governor, is calling for companies and the government to do more to orient American workers in a new A.I. era.

item.title: Lesson Learned From Iran War: Reduce Reliance on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Increased stockpiles, more pipelines and other tactics could loosen the waterway’s stranglehold on the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:description: A vessel in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, this week.

item.title: U.S. Inflation Problems Are Far From Over
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s new chairman has vowed to deliver price stability, but officials are at odds over whether that will require higher borrowing costs.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the new chairman of the Federal Reserve, has expressed interest in examining how it measures and models price pressures.

item.title: How Honda’s Pledge to Go All-Electric Unraveled
item.description: A derailed gamble led Honda to its first-ever annual loss and has intensified scrutiny over the future of its chief executive.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:description: Honda was developing several next-generation electric vehicles that were ultimately scrapped because of declining global E.V. demand.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After Attack Halts Shipping in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Oil prices fell, moving closer to levels not seen since the war in Iran began in February.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: She Stole a Knicks Trash Can Off the Street and Lost Her Job at JPMorgan
item.description: After a video of Angie Baez taking a Knicks-themed trash can went viral, a JPMorgan Chase spokeswoman said she was no longer with the company.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman and Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Knicks’ championship parade last week in Manhattan.

item.title: With a Proposed $250 Bill, Trump Reimagines America by Putting Himself on Its Money
item.description: Cash might be a relic, but — like royalty and emperors — President Trump still wants his face on American currency.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport

item.title: Norma Yaeger, Barrier-Breaking Stockbroker, Dies at 96
item.description: Over her husband’s objections, she became a Wall Street broker in the 1960s. She later started two securities firms in the overwhelmingly male financial world.
item.dc:creator: James R. Hagerty

item.title: Airbus Is Ordered to Inspect 16 Jets After Cracks Are Found in Wings
item.description: The European Union’s chief aviation regulator ordered five A380 superjumbo jets to be grounded immediately.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:description: An Airbus A380 flying at Paris-Le Bourget Airport in 2017.

item.title: Why the Fed Could Loom Large Over the Midterms
item.description: Wall Street increasingly predicts the central bank will raise interest rates, possibly ahead of Election Day in November. That could anger President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The odds are rising on Wall Street that the Fed will raise interest rates, perhaps before Election Day.
